hero.tagline: West Virginia's Magistrate Courts handle small claims up to $10,000 with streamlined procedures for quick resolutions.

at_a_glance.label: Most you can sue for
at_a_glance.detail: Same cap for individuals and businesses

at_a_glance.label: Appeal window
at_a_glance.detail: Trial de novo in Circuit Court

at_a_glance.label: Recent change
at_a_glance.detail: No major changes in the last 3 years

limits.split_claims_explanation: If your claim exceeds $10,000, you must file in Circuit Court; splitting a claim to fit the limit is not permitted.

claim_categories.title: Contracts and money owed
claim_categories.blurb: West Virginia allows you to file claims up to $10,000 for disputes involving contracts and money owed. The statute of limitations varies depending on the type of contract.

claims.name: Breach of a written contract
claims.example: A contractor signed a $4,000 contract to remodel your kitchen, took the deposit, and never finished.
claims.notes: 10-year deadline from breach. Bring the signed contract, payment proof, and any correspondence showing the breach.

claims.name: Breach of an oral contract
claims.example: You agreed verbally to lend someone $2,000, and they failed to repay.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from breach. Gather any evidence of the agreement, such as emails or witness statements.

claims.name: Unpaid personal loan
claims.example: You loaned a friend $3,000 with the promise of repayment in six months, but they haven't paid.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from breach. Provide documentation of the loan agreement and any repayment attempts.

claims.name: Default on a promissory note
claims.example: A borrower signed a promissory note for $5,000 and failed to make the agreed payments.
claims.notes: 10-year deadline from breach. Present the original promissory note and records of missed payments.

claims.name: Unpaid invoice for services rendered
claims.example: You provided $1,500 worth of consulting services, but the client hasn't paid the invoice.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from breach. Submit the invoice, service agreement, and any communication regarding payment.

claims.name: Unpaid balance on an open account
claims.example: A customer has an outstanding balance of $2,000 on their account with your business.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from breach. Provide account statements and records of transactions.

claims.name: Unpaid rent by tenant
claims.example: Your tenant owes $3,500 in unpaid rent over several months.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from breach. Bring the lease agreement, rent ledger, and any notices sent to the tenant.

claims.name: Payment with a bad check
claims.example: A customer paid for $800 worth of goods with a check that bounced.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from the date of the bad check. Provide the returned check and any related correspondence.
claims.damage_boost: Check amount + service charge up to $25 + civil penalty up to $100 OR 2x check amount, whichever is greater.

claims.name: Co-signer seeking repayment
claims.example: You co-signed a $7,000 loan, and the primary borrower defaulted, leaving you responsible.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from breach. Present the loan agreement and proof of payments made on behalf of the borrower.

claim_categories.title: Consumer disputes
claim_categories.blurb: West Virginia's small claims courts handle various consumer disputes up to $10,000, with a 2-year statute of limitations for most cases.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective product
claims.example: You bought a $1,200 appliance that stopped working within a month, and the seller refuses to refund or replace it.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from the date of purchase. Bring the receipt, warranty, and any communication with the seller.

claims.name: Substandard service provided
claims.example: A cleaning service charged you $500 but did not perform the agreed-upon tasks.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from the date of service. Provide the service agreement, payment proof, and photos of the incomplete work.

claims.name: Unsatisfactory auto repair
claims.example: A mechanic charged $2,000 for repairs that did not fix your car's issue.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from the date of repair. Bring the repair invoice, payment proof, and any subsequent repair records.

claims.name: Contractor failed to complete work
claims.example: You paid a contractor $8,000 to renovate your bathroom, but they abandoned the project halfway.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from the date of breach. Provide the contract, payment records, and photos of incomplete work.

claims.name: Vendor failed to deliver goods/services
claims.example: You paid $1,500 for catering services, but the vendor never showed up on the event day.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from the date of the event. Bring the contract, payment proof, and any communication with the vendor.

claims.name: Unauthorized subscription charges after cancellation
claims.example: You canceled a $50/month subscription, but the company continued to charge your account.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from the date of unauthorized charge. Provide cancellation confirmation and bank statements showing charges.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective used car
claims.example: You bought a used car for $9,000 that broke down within a week, and the dealer refuses to address the issue.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from the date of purchase. Bring the sales contract, payment proof, and mechanic's report.

claims.name: False advertising leading to financial loss
claims.example: A company advertised a product for $200 as 'waterproof,' but it was not, causing damage to your property.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from the date of purchase. Provide the advertisement, receipt, and evidence of the product's failure.

claim_categories.title: Property damage
claim_categories.blurb: West Virginia's small claims courts handle property damage claims up to $10,000, with a 2-year statute of limitations.

claims.name: Damage from a vehicle collision
claims.example: Another driver hit your parked car, causing $3,000 in damages.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from the date of the accident. Bring the police report, repair estimates, and photos of the damage.

claims.name: Neighbor's tree damaged your property
claims.example: A neighbor's tree fell onto your fence during a storm, causing $1,500 in damages.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from the date of damage. Provide photos, repair estimates, and any communication with the neighbor.

claims.name: Tenant caused damage to rental property
claims.example: A tenant left your rental unit with $4,000 in damages beyond normal wear and tear.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from the date of discovery. Bring the lease agreement, photos of damages, and repair invoices.

claims.name: Neighbor's pet caused damage to your property
claims.example: A neighbor's dog dug up your garden, causing $800 in damages.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from the date of damage. Provide photos, repair estimates, and any communication with the pet owner.

claims.name: Damage to property left in someone's care
claims.example: You left your bicycle with a friend, and they returned it with $600 in damages.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from the date of damage. Provide evidence of the agreement and photos of the damage.

claim_categories.title: Landlord-tenant disputes
claim_categories.blurb: West Virginia's small claims courts handle various landlord-tenant disputes up to $10,000, with specific statutes governing each type.

claims.name: Failure to return security deposit
claims.example: Your landlord withheld your $1,200 security deposit without justification.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from the end of tenancy. Provide the lease agreement, move-out inspection report, and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Landlord failed to provide itemized deductions from deposit
claims.example: Your landlord deducted $500 from your deposit without providing an itemized list of damages.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from the end of tenancy. Bring the lease agreement and any correspondence regarding the deposit.

claims.name: Landlord failed to maintain habitable conditions
claims.example: Your apartment had no heating during winter, and the landlord refused to fix it.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from the date of issue. Provide photos, repair requests, and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Landlord wrongfully retained tenant's property
claims.example: After eviction, your landlord kept your furniture worth $3,000.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from the date of retention. Provide the lease agreement and inventory of retained items.

claims.name: Landlord conducted an illegal eviction
claims.example: Your landlord changed the locks without notice, preventing you from accessing your apartment.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from the date of eviction. Provide the lease agreement and any communication regarding the eviction.

claims.name: Landlord failed to return prepaid rent after termination
claims.example: You paid first and last month's rent, but the landlord didn't return the last month's rent after early termination.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from the end of tenancy. Provide the lease agreement and proof of prepaid rent.

claims.name: Tenant failed to pay rent
claims.example: Your tenant owes $2,500 in unpaid rent over several months.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from the date of last unpaid rent. Bring the lease agreement and rent ledger.

claims.name: Tenant repaired and deducted from rent
claims.example: You paid $600 to fix a leaking roof after the landlord refused, and deducted it from rent.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from the date of repair. Provide repair receipts and communication with the landlord.

claim_categories.title: Employment and wage disputes
claim_categories.blurb: West Virginia's small claims courts handle employment and wage disputes up to $10,000, with a 2-year statute of limitations for most claims.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned wages
claims.example: Your employer owes you $1,500 for hours worked but not paid.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from the date wages were due. Provide pay stubs and records of hours worked.

claims.name: Employer failed to provide final paycheck
claims.example: After termination, your employer didn't give you your final paycheck of $2,000.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from the date final wages were due. Provide termination notice and records of final wages owed.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned commissions
claims.example: You earned $3,500 in sales commissions that your employer hasn't paid.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from the date commissions were due. Provide sales records and commission agreements.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay overtime wages
claims.example: You worked 10 hours of overtime but weren't paid the additional $300 owed.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from the date overtime was worked. Provide timesheets and pay records.

claim_categories.title: Personal claims
claim_categories.blurb: West Virginia's small claims courts handle various personal claims up to $10,000, with a 2-year statute of limitations for most cases.

claims.name: Minor personal injury
claims.example: You slipped on a wet floor in a store, resulting in $2,000 in medical bills.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from the date of injury. Provide medical records and any incident reports.

claims.name: Failure to return loaned property
claims.example: You loaned a friend your $1,000 camera, and they refuse to return it.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from the date of refusal. Provide evidence of the loan agreement and any communication.

claims.name: Theft of personal property
claims.example: Someone stole your bicycle worth $800, and you know their identity.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from the date of theft. Provide police reports and any evidence of ownership.

claim_categories.title: Vehicle-specific disputes
claim_categories.blurb: West Virginia's small claims courts handle vehicle-related disputes up to $10,000, with a 2-year statute of limitations for most cases.

claims.name: Dispute over mechanic's repair work
claims.example: A mechanic charged $2,500 for repairs that didn't fix your car's issue.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from the date of repair. Provide repair invoices and any subsequent repair records.

claims.name: Vehicle was wrongfully towed
claims.example: Your car was towed from a legal parking spot, resulting in $300 in fees.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from the date of towing. Provide towing receipts and photos of the parking area.

exclusions.explanation: Magistrate Courts do not have jurisdiction over cases involving the title to real estate.
Defamation cases are excluded from Magistrate Court jurisdiction.
Magistrate Courts do not handle cases involving malicious prosecution.
Cases involving eminent domain are outside the jurisdiction of Magistrate Courts.
Divorce, child custody, and other family law matters are not handled in Magistrate Court.
Magistrate Courts do not have jurisdiction over probate matters.
Claims against the federal government are not within the jurisdiction of state courts.
Workers' compensation claims are handled by the Workers' Compensation Board, not Magistrate Courts.
Magistrate Courts do not handle class action lawsuits.
Magistrate Courts cannot grant injunctions or order specific performance.

damages.punitive_explanation: Magistrate Courts do not award punitive damages; however, statutory penalties may be available in certain cases.

statutory_multipliers.multiplier: 2x check amount or check amount + service charge up to $25 + civil penalty up to $100, whichever is greater
statutory_multipliers.explanation: Check amount + service charge up to $25 + civil penalty up to $100 OR 2x check amount, whichever is greater.

damages.attorney_fees_explanation: Attorney fees may be recoverable in certain cases, such as those involving bad checks or violations of the Consumer Credit and Protection Act.

filing_options: County where the defendant resides or can be served
Any county where one defendant resides
Where the cause of action arose

where_to_file.consequences_of_wrong_venue: Filing in the wrong venue may result in dismissal or transfer of the case, potentially causing delays and additional costs.
where_to_file.e_filing_notes: E-filing availability varies by county; check with the local Magistrate Court for options.

service_fees.notes: Available as an alternative to personal service.

eligibility_bullets: Receiving need-based public assistance (SNAP, TANF, SSI)
Household income below 125% federal poverty line
waiver.coverage_notes: The waiver covers filing fees and service fees.
waiver.if_you_win_notes: If you win, the waived fees are added to the judgment against the loser.

fees.fees_recoverable_from_loser_notes: If you win, filing fees and reasonable service costs are added to the judgment.

entries.clock_starts: Date of last payment or charge

statute_of_limitations.discovery_rule_notes: The discovery rule applies to fraud claims, starting the clock when the fraud is discovered.
statute_of_limitations.tolling_notes: The statute of limitations may be tolled if the defendant is out of state, the plaintiff is a minor, or in cases of bankruptcy.

pre_filing.demand_letter_notes: While not required, sending a demand letter can often resolve disputes without court intervention.
pre_filing.bad_check_notice_rule: For bad checks, a 30-day written notice demanding payment is required before filing a claim.
pre_filing.wage_claim_demand_rule: No specific pre-filing demand required for wage claims, but it's advisable to request payment in writing.
pre_filing.landlord_tenant_pre_filing_notes: Landlords must provide an itemized list of damages within 30 days of tenancy termination when withholding a security deposit.
pre_filing.consumer_protection_notes: No specific pre-filing requirements for consumer protection claims, but documenting all communications is recommended.

forms.name: Civil Complaint
forms.description: Initiates a civil case in Magistrate Court.

forms.name: Answer
forms.description: Filed by the defendant to respond to a complaint.

forms.name: Affidavit of Default
forms.description: Used to request a default judgment when the defendant fails to respond.

forms.name: Financial Affidavit
forms.description: Used to request a waiver of court fees due to financial hardship.

methods.name: Sheriff personal service
methods.description: Sheriff or constable personally hands the papers to the defendant.

methods.name: Certified mail by clerk
methods.description: Clerk sends the documents via certified mail to the defendant.

methods.name: Private process server
methods.description: A private individual authorized to serve legal documents delivers the papers to the defendant.

service.business_service_rule: Serve the registered agent or an officer of the corporation; for sole proprietors, serve the individual directly.
service.out_of_state_defendant_notes: Service must comply with West Virginia's long-arm statute and the rules of the state where service is made.
service.cant_find_defendant_notes: If the defendant cannot be located, service by publication may be permitted with court approval.
service.avoiding_service_notes: If the defendant is evading service, document attempts and seek court permission for alternative methods.

response_and_default.response_notes: The defendant must file a written answer within 20 days of service; failure to do so may result in a default judgment.
response_and_default.default_process: If the defendant fails to respond or appear, the court may grant a default judgment in favor of the plaintiff.
response_and_default.prove_up_notes: Even in default cases, the plaintiff must provide evidence to support the claim amount.

motion_to_vacate_form.name: Motion to Set Aside Default Judgment

response_and_default.motion_to_vacate_appeal_notes: A motion to vacate must be filed within 20 days; if denied, an appeal can be filed within 20 days of the denial.

hearing.lawyers_allowed_notes: Parties may represent themselves or be represented by an attorney.
hearing.format: Bench trial with informal procedures; each party presents their case, and the magistrate makes a decision.
what_to_bring: Names and contact info for witnesses
hearing.interpreter_request_process: Parties must arrange for their own interpreters; the court does not provide them.
hearing.decision_timing: On the spot or mailed within a few weeks

appeals.type_notes: The Circuit Court conducts a new trial as if the Magistrate Court trial had not occurred.
appeals.default_judgment_notes: A default judgment can be appealed within 20 days; the appeal results in a new trial in Circuit Court.

methods.name: Wage garnishment
methods.blurb: Have a portion of the debtor's wages withheld until the judgment is paid.
methods.description: File a writ of garnishment with the court, serve the employer, and the employer withholds a portion of the debtor's wages to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor is employed and earns above the exemption threshold.

methods.name: Bank levy
methods.blurb: Seize funds from the debtor's bank account to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of execution from the court, serve it on the bank, and the bank freezes and turns over funds to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor has sufficient funds in the account; some funds may be exempt.

methods.name: Property lien
methods.blurb: Place a lien on the debtor's real property to secure the judgment amount.
methods.description: File the judgment with the county clerk to create a lien on the debtor's property; the lien must be satisfied before the property can be sold or refinanced.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor owns real property; may take time to realize payment.

methods.name: Writ of execution
methods.blurb: Seize and sell the debtor's non-exempt personal property to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of execution from the court, the sheriff seizes non-exempt property, sells it at auction, and applies proceeds to the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor owns valuable non-exempt personal property.

collection.judgment_renewal_process: File a petition for renewal before the judgment expires; a hearing may be required.
collection.bankruptcy_effect_notes: Filing for bankruptcy may discharge the judgment; certain debts are non-dischargeable.
collection.priority_notes: Priority among creditors is generally determined by the order in which liens are filed.

businesses.self_representation_notes: Businesses may represent themselves in Magistrate Court without an attorney.
businesses.fictitious_name_notes: Businesses operating under a trade name must file a Certificate of Assumed Name with the county clerk.
businesses.sole_proprietor_employee_exception: Sole proprietors may authorize an employee to represent them in court.
businesses.licensing_notes: Unlicensed contractors may be barred from suing to recover fees for work requiring a license.

pitfalls.title: Incorrect defendant information
pitfalls.what_happens: Suing the wrong party or using incorrect names can result in dismissal or unenforceable judgments.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Verify the exact legal name and address of the defendant before filing; for businesses, check with the Secretary of State.

pitfalls.title: Missing statute of limitations
pitfalls.what_happens: Filing after the statute of limitations expires leads to automatic dismissal.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Confirm the applicable statute of limitations for your claim type before filing.

pitfalls.title: Improper service of process
pitfalls.what_happens: Failure to serve the defendant correctly can delay the case or result in dismissal.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Follow the court's rules for service of process precisely; consider using the sheriff's office for service.

pitfalls.title: Insufficient evidence
pitfalls.what_happens: Lack of evidence can lead to losing the case, even if your claim is valid.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Gather all relevant documents, photos, and witness statements before the hearing.

pitfalls.title: Ignoring counterclaims
pitfalls.what_happens: If the defendant files a counterclaim and you don't respond, you could lose by default.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Respond to any counterclaims promptly and prepare your defense.

pitfalls.title: Failing to collect judgment
pitfalls.what_happens: Winning a judgment doesn't guarantee payment; you must take steps to collect.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Learn about collection methods like garnishment and liens; act promptly to enforce the judgment.

faqs.question: Do I need a lawyer?
faqs.answer: No, you are not required to have a lawyer in Magistrate Court; individuals and businesses can represent themselves.

faqs.question: How long does it take?
faqs.answer: The timeline varies, but most small claims cases are resolved within 30 to 90 days from filing to judgment.

faqs.question: What's the maximum I can sue for?
faqs.answer: You can sue for up to $10,000 in Magistrate Court; claims exceeding this amount must be filed in Circuit Court.

faqs.question: What happens if the defendant doesn't show up?
faqs.answer: If the defendant fails to appear, the court may grant a default judgment in your favor.

faqs.question: Can I appeal?
faqs.answer: Yes, either party can appeal a Magistrate Court decision to the Circuit Court within 20 days for a trial de novo.

faqs.question: How long is a judgment good for?
faqs.answer: A judgment is valid for 10 years and can be renewed before it expires.

state_specific_notes: West Virginia allows evictions to be filed in Magistrate Court regardless of the amount in controversy.
Magistrate Courts do not have jurisdiction over cases involving equitable relief.
Defendants have 20 days to file an answer after being served with a complaint.
Either party can request a jury trial in Magistrate Court if the amount in controversy exceeds $20.
Appeals from Magistrate Court are heard de novo in Circuit Court, meaning a new trial is conducted.

interesting_findings: West Virginia's small claims limit of $10,000 is higher than many neighboring states, providing broader access to simplified court procedures for larger disputes.
